name: Ethiopian Landrace

migrationPath.notes: Origin of all Coffea arabica; contains the greatest genetic diversity of any coffee-growing region

notableProducers: Guduba wet mill (Benti Nenka, Hambela Wamena, Guji — 1925–2110m, 589 smallholders)
Harmufo (Gedeb, Yirgachefe — 2300–2400m; honey processed; first isolated as standalone lot 2025)
description: Ethiopia is the birthplace of all Coffea arabica. DNA testing has confirmed over 60 distinct varieties growing in Ethiopia, making it home to the most coffee biodiversity of any region in the world. Most Ethiopian coffees sold commercially are blends of multiple native varieties — the term 'Ethiopian Landrace' is used when genetic testing confirms Ethiopian origin but the database is not yet large enough to identify the specific variety or accession. Single-variety lots are rare but emerging: microregion isolation is increasingly possible through partnerships between washing stations and exporters. Several high-value Colombian varieties (Pink Bourbon, Chiroso, Sidra) are themselves classified as Ethiopian Landrace — they likely traveled to Latin America through undocumented routes. The variety also appears in Burundian field blends alongside Bourbon and Mibirizi.
